aboutme.title: About me
aboutme.description:
Hi! I’m Rubén, a video game programmer born in Spain and passionate about the development and the creation of interactive experiences. I consider myself an extremely curious person, perseverant and motivated to improve my skills as a developer.

I graduated from U-tad with both a Bachelor’s degree in Design and Development of videogames and a Master’s specialized in code. I started at the age of 12 learning to use the Unity 3D engine self-taught, being highly proficient in it today, as well as in other engines, languages and tools (Unreal Engine, Godot, Lua, OpenGL, etc).

This training and background have allowed me to participate in so many different projects, where I could work as a Gameplay Programmer or take the responsibility of a Lead Programmer, managing developer teams in professional environments.
projects.title: Projects
contact.title: Contact
contact.name: Rubén Santos Cascos
contact.description: For any inquiries, you can contact me at: scruben027@gmail.com. I will get back to you as soon as possible! You can also find me on my social media.
project.card.bluey: Gameplay and Lead Programmer at Artax Games, working alongside Outright Games and Ludo Studio (creators of the Disney+ series). I was responsible for the game architecture, the core mechanics and team coordination.
project.card.dusty: Gameplay and Animation Programmer at El Palo Games, where I had the opportunity to dive deep into different Unreal systems and explore other programming areas.
project.card.motor3d: Personal project developed in C++ and OpenGL as a sandbox to understand low-level engine architecture, managing the graphics pipeline, mesh rendering and shaders.
project.card.moonchild: Academic team project where I took on the role of Programmer and Producer. It was my first team project where I could experience the entire video game development process.
project.card.superpanas: Local co-op game developed in less than 3 days for a Game Jam. Fully coded in Unity, focused on fun arcade mechanics and polished controls for two simultaneous players.
project.bluey.title: Bluey: The Videogame
project.bluey.subtitle.project.type: Project type: Professional - Comercial video game development
project.bluey.about.one: During my work on Bluey: The Videogame, I started as a Gameplay Programmer during the first year of production. Thanks to my performance and vision for the project, I later took on the role of Lead Programmer. To this day, it is the most ambitious milestone of my career, and it has definitely helped me improve both my programming logic and my team leadership skills, as well as optimizing workflows between different departments.
project.bluey.about.two:
My work on the project was divided into two main stages:

  • Gameplay Programmer Stage: Design and implementation of core player-focused mechanics (player controller and game feel), development of minigames, and architecture of the main episode and progression system.
  • Lead Programmer Stage: Management of the programming team, time estimation, task assignment and review, and solving technical blockers, while continuing to actively contribute to the development of new game code.

During the final phase of the project, I led the porting and optimization process for consoles (PS4, PS5, Nintendo Switch, Xbox One, and Xbox Series X/S). In this stage, I fixed hardware-specific bugs (rendering, save system, audio, etc.) and implemented native features required for build approval, such as PlayStation 5 Activities and adapting the Nintendo Switch control scheme for both handheld and docked modes.

All the information detailed below covers the full development cycle up to the release day, as I later left the company to continue my studies. Because of this, I may mention features that are no longer in the game or have been modified. Since the source code is under confidentiality and intellectual property agreements, I do not have access to it, so I will illustrate the parts of the project I remember using pseudocode blocks.
project.bluey.video.description: Play a brand-new story set across 4 interactive adventures, explore iconic locations, play your favourite games from the TV show and also play with your friends and family in local multiplayer.
project.bluey.accordion.one.title: Episode system +
project.bluey.accordion.one.content.one:
I was responsible for both the architecture design and the technical implementation of the episode system. This feature was decided midway through development without any previous foundation in the project, so I took on the responsibility of creating a modular system in parallel with the work of the narrative design team.

From the beginning, I approached the tool with a data-driven design mindset, allowing high flexibility so that designers, animators, and other programmers could iterate on content directly from the Unity editor, reducing the need for code when creating new missions.

On a technical level, each episode was encapsulated in a ScriptableObject (EpisodeSO) as an independent data container, which was centrally managed by an EpisodeManager. Each EpisodeSO structured its flow through an ordered list of checkpoints, while also storing all essential information such as titles, visual rewards, unlockable minigames, and more.
project.bluey.accordion.one.content.two:
The checkpoints were structured using polymorphism with ScriptableObject, divided into two main types inheriting from a common class (CheckPointBase): CinematicCheckpoint and MissionCheckpoint.

The CheckPointBase class contained the variables and methods shared by any episode node. Its main advantage was automating the checkpoint lifecycle: it handled asynchronous scene loading, ambient music, player positioning, game state save persistence, and the instantiation and destruction of required objects.
project.bluey.accordion.one.content.thre: On one hand, CinematicCheckPoint included a PlayableAsset (the sequence clip), which was played in a centralized way by the CinematicManager. This node allowed configuring whether the scene required a traditional cinematic format (black bars) or if it was an interactive in-game sequence, where animators could modify the environment in real time (for example, equipping tools or hiding UI elements and props):
project.bluey.accordion.one.content.four: On the other hand, MissionCheckPoint represented the core gameplay of the episode. Through this system, it was possible to initialize minigames or structure dynamic objectives, such as moving between points of interest or interacting with elements in the environment. It also allowed configuring AI behavior trees for that specific node, enabling an NPC to guide the player organically, react to their actions, or move autonomously through the scenario:
project.bluey.accordion.one.content.five:
Finally, the EpisodeManager was responsible for managing the full episode flow. Its responsibilities included starting episodes, controlling checkpoint progression through identifiers, communicating with other systems, and unlocking new content upon completion.

It also handled save game resumption. If a player left an episode unfinished, the manager rebuilt the game state by iterating through the checkpoints list in the EpisodeSO, repositioning characters, restoring the music, and re-instantiating the necessary objects in the scene.
project.bluey.accordion.two.title: Cinematics system +
project.bluey.accordion.two.content.one:
Within the cinematic system, I implemented several key elements focused on visual presentation, accessibility, and usability for the development team:

  • Cinematic components: I developed the black bars system with its corresponding in/out animations for narrative transitions.
  • Custom Timeline: I created a custom audio track for Unity’s cinematic editor, so designers only needed to input the identifier of an audio clip for the system to automatically play the voice line along with its associated subtitles, optimizing development time and allowing easy language switching.
  • Modular Subtitles and Accessibility: I designed a fully configurable subtitle system based on Scriptable Objects. It allowed the design team to adapt text color depending on the speaking character, apply dynamic modifiers using Rich Text, adjust character reveal speed, and manage localization for different languages.
project.bluey.accordion.three.title: Activities system +
project.bluey.accordion.three.content.on:
I was responsible for implementing and maintaining the activity manager. These activities were minigames that players could unlock as they progressed through episodes and could later play in different areas of the maps.

There were four minigames inheriting from the abstract class ActivityBase, which contained the common methods and variables for all activities (time management, participating NPCs, win conditions, player teleportation, and objects to instantiate). These minigames were fully configurable by design using Scriptable Objects, which were interpreted by the ActivityManager.

Additionally, I unified the interface of all minigames so it could be easily controlled through a shared event system, regardless of which minigame was active at any given time.
project.bluey.accordion.four.title: Character actions +
project.bluey.accordion.four.content.one:
I was responsible for iterating on and polishing character actions, mainly focusing on:

  • Base movement and jumping.
  • Logic for picking up, throwing, and dropping objects.
  • Interaction for watering plants.
  • Mechanics for getting on/off parents and controlling their movement.
  • Physics and interaction for hitting the balloon (the Keepy Uppy minigame).
  • Crawling action (eventually discarded).

All these actions were managed through a custom state machine. The system determined allowed transitions by evaluating filters in real time, such as the character’s current state, nearby interactable objects, or elements within their direct line of sight.

This system was implemented for both the player and the AI, as NPCs were able to perform the same actions as the player within the game world.
project.bluey.accordion.five.title: Troubleshooting & Tooling +
project.bluey.accordion.five.content.one:
During development, several technical issues and production bottlenecks arose. To optimize the workflow across different departments, I designed and developed various internal tools in Unity, including:

  • Debug HUD for QA: I implemented a real-time debug interface to facilitate bug detection and reporting. This panel clearly displayed controller inputs, timestamp information, the exact build version, and critical system parameters.
  • Cinematic playback tool: I designed a utility for the animation team that allowed them to test sequences in isolation as soon as the game started. After identifying that animators were spending a significant amount of time playing through full episodes to review a specific scene, I created this system to recreate the required gameplay context and instantly play the cinematic.
  • Editor shortcuts: I integrated custom buttons into the Unity toolbar to load any scene in the project with a single click. This significantly improved navigation and daily testing for the modeling and level design teams.
  • FMOD reset tool: I implemented a button in the Unity toolbar to fix a recurring issue with the FMOD integration that corrupted audio after a crash. Instead of forcing the team to fully restart the engine, this tool reset the sound API directly, significantly reducing the impact of the issue.
project.bluey.accordion.six.title: Animated UI & HUDs +
project.bluey.accordion.six.content.one:
I was responsible for several key gameplay interfaces, including the toy inventory, the character customization system, the episode selection menu, the Sticker Book, and the different HUDs for the minigames.

The episode selection menus and the Sticker Book featured a large number of dynamic animations fully managed through code, strategically designed to guide the user through the interface in an intuitive way.

To achieve an organic and fluid experience, I implemented custom easing functions for all entry and exit transitions. My goal was to apply different Game Feel principles to give the UI a much more interactive and juicy finish.
project.bluey.accordion.seven.title: AI +
project.bluey.accordion.seven.content.on:
We developed a modular AI system based on Behavior Trees, which allowed us to structure character logic flexibly depending on the context of the gameplay.

For example, when starting a minigame, the AI would adopt the specific mechanics of that activity to compete or cooperate as a real player. During narrative episodes, NPCs were dynamically configured with different roles, ranging from acting as mission guides to behaving as organic companions to the player.

I adapted many of the base actions to be compatible with the AI, which allowed us to build a much more dynamic and living world, ensuring that the player always felt accompanied even in single-player sessions.

Additionally, this system helped solve critical design and gameplay issues. In co-op mode, if a player moved out of the camera frame and got stuck, the system would temporarily transfer control to the AI to reposition them safely back into view. Once repositioned, control was immediately returned to the player, completely removing a major source of frustration.
project.bluey.accordion.eight.title: Camera +
project.bluey.accordion.eight.content.on:
I was responsible for designing and implementing the co-op camera system to smoothly manage the visualization of multiple players on a single screen. After several tests, I opted for a system based on calculating the midpoint between all players (excluding NPCs), where each player contributed a variable weight depending on the gameplay situation to smooth the framing.

To achieve framing similar to the animated series, I integrated a secondary rail-based camera system in specific areas of the map. Through triggers, the system detected when the players’ midpoint entered these areas and automatically activated fixed or dynamic rail-based framing.

The entire architecture was built on top of Cinemachine, leveraging its native capabilities for camera tracking and transitions. This allowed us to compose highly precise shots, efficiently manage camera transitions, and ensure a visual result identical to the series.
project.dusty.title: Dusty
project.dusty.subtitle.software.used: Software used: Unreal Engine and Visual Studio
project.dusty.about.one:
On Dusty, I participated as a gameplay and animation programmer during 2024, as part of the Master's Degree in Game Programming at U-TAD. The project was developed by a team of approximately 30 people, including 7 programmers.

Thanks to this project, I was able to dive deeper into Unreal Engine, familiarizing myself with its tools and systems, while improving my C++ skills applied to game development.

In addition to contributing to development, we had the opportunity to experience the entire publishing and presentation process. Dusty was showcased at events such as the Guerrilla Game Festival and the BIG (Bilbao International Games Conference). It was also nominated for the PlayStation Talents awards in the categories of “Best Game of the Year”, “Best Press Game”, and “Best Art”, and was featured in specialized media outlets like Hobby Consolas, which gave us firsthand experience in presenting a video game to both the public and the industry.
project.dusty.video.description: Dusty is a third-person puzzle game focused on explore beautiful environments. Join archaeologist Dusty on his search for a mysterious civilization. Explore equipped with your extensible cane and notebook to resolve the past, the ways of the civilization and his technology of light.
project.dusty.accordion.one.title: Finite State Machine +
project.dusty.accordion.one.content.one:
I developed a Finite State Machine (FSM) designed to be used by any game object that required it (characters, puzzles, Dusty's staff with its different actions, etc.), although it was ultimately only utilized for the player. The state machine is straightforward: it features an ordered list of states the player can be in, and each of these states includes another list of states to which it can transition.

This action map was stored within a DataAsset, allowing anyone to edit it directly from the engine, while the action filter managed the current state and evaluated whether a transition to another state was permitted based on the DataAsset configuration.

Example of the class inheritance structure for the player's grounded movement state:
project.dusty.accordion.one.content.two: This is the action filter script. Each entity is responsible for initializing it with its corresponding states DataAsset and its initial state, as well as requesting state transitions.
project.dusty.accordion.one.content.thre: This is the DataAsset responsible for linking the current state with its available transition states.
project.dusty.accordion.two.title: Animation Blueprint Instance +
project.dusty.accordion.two.content.one: I was in charge of implementing Dusty's animations and linking them to the action state machine. To achieve this, I created an Anim Instance and two animation state machines: one for the character's natural movement and a strafe machine for when the player is aiming. I also ensured that switching between these state machines used a smooth transition to improve the aiming feel.
project.dusty.accordion.two.content.two: BaseLocomotion handles the character's ground movement animations, as well as those for jumping and airborne states. Animations related to staff actions were triggered via an Animation Montage.
project.dusty.accordion.two.content.thre: StrafeLocomotion contains the character's grounded movement animations while aiming with the staff.
project.dusty.accordion.two.content.four: I added an Inertialization node to achieve a more natural transition that respects the character's inertia. Additionally, I implemented lean animations when turning sideways and a subtle secondary animation overlap during fast aiming and quick turns.
project.dusty.accordion.three.title: IK Rig +
project.dusty.accordion.three.content.on: Since the game's terrain is highly irregular, and to add an extra layer of attention to detail, I decided to implement an IK Rig for the character's legs, preventing them from floating in the air on irregular surfaces.
project.dusty.accordion.three.content.tw: I also added another IK Rig for the head, allowing Dusty to look at points or objects of interest and redirect the player's attention.
project.dusty.accordion.three.content.th: Finally, I added a subtle overlap effect to Dusty's mustache to bring a bit more life to the character.
project.dusty.accordion.four.title: Others +
project.dusty.accordion.four.content.one:
Although my role in the project was focused on the aforementioned areas, I also worked directly on other parts of the project, such as:

  • Character movement and actions involving the extendable staff
  • Physics and collision systems for the extendable staff
  • Interactable objects, such as vines or magnetic cubes
  • Puzzle implementation (the academy puzzle and rotating bases)
  • Integration of particles and special effects (staff effects or camera shakes)
  • Door logic, animation, and visual effects
  • Cinematics system
  • General bug fixing
  • Collaboration with the design team on specific sections of the game
project.motor3d.title: 3D Engine
project.motor3d.about.one:
My 3D Engine is a personal, self-taught project born with the objective of diving deeper into and understanding modern video game engine architecture. Starting from the academic foundation provided during my 3D Engines course, I have continued expanding this graphics engine on my own in C++ to improve my low-level memory management skills, applied mathematics, and rendering pipelines.

The core pillars I established upon completing my Master's degree were:

  • Graphics Mathematics: Interpretation and application of linear algebra and matrix transformations (MVP matrices: Model-View-Projection).
  • Graphics Pipeline with OpenGL: Configuration of the graphics context, buffer management (VBO, VAO, EBO), and an introduction to shaders.
  • Structure and Patterns: Implementation of a decoupled game loop, a clean and modular rendering architecture, and the ECS (Entity Component System) pattern to guarantee high performance and scalability.
  • Loading and Unloading: Loading 3D meshes along with their respective materials and textures.
project.motor3d.about.two:
Later, I decided to take the engine one step further to explore more complex implementations:

  • PBR Rendering (Physically Based Rendering): Simulation of realistic materials based on the physical properties of light.
  • Dynamic Environment: Rendering of skyboxes along with directional, point, and ambient lighting.
  • Audio and Resource System: Integration of OpenAL featuring the Doppler Effect, and development of a resource manager for loading textures and materials.
project.motor3d.about.three: I use this engine as a sandbox where I experiment with low-level technologies, and although right now I don’t plan on publishing it, I don’t rule out doing so in the future once it is more complete. For now, my next objective is to refactor the graphics system that I have with OpenGL to better understand how it works under the hood, since that was a highly guided process during university.
project.moonchild.title: Moon Child
project.moonchild.about.one: Moonchild was developed in 2021 and represented my first major real-world experience working in a multidisciplinary team, taking on the roles of Solo Programmer and Producer of the DynamiStudios team (composed of 6 people). This project marked a turning point in my career, because it allowed me to lead a team, handle the planning and manage the complete development workflow up to release.
project.moonchild.about.two:
The game was developed in 4 months. Although analyzing my code from that time is a reminder of how much I have evolved technically, the process provided an invaluable learning experience. During this period, I designed and implemented all the core systems:

  • Gameplay and progression: Character movement system, health management, skills, interactable objects, and player progression.
  • AI: Behavior and logic for 3 different types of enemies.
  • Visual effects: Implementation of visual effects (VFX), dynamic particles, and interaction with materials at runtime.
  • Dynamic audio: Physical surface detection for character footsteps and changing music tracks at different moments of the game.
  • Narrative: Cinematic system, implementation of animations, and animated subtitles.
  • Game flow: Save system and menus.
  • Art and Design: Direct collaboration with the creative direction and 3D modeling of environment assets like houses or platforms.

The final result exceeded our expectations considering the limited time and knowledge we had at the time. All of the team received a Grade of Excellence for our work, accompanied by a special commendation from our project supervisor.

I share the email that we received at that moment, one of my biggest sources of motivation for creating new experiences and inspiring my way in this amazing industry.
project.moonchild.video.description: Moon Child is a 2.5D platformer in a fantasy world, where players embody a God of unknown origin. Their mission is to deliver one of the villagers’ children to complete the lunar cycle, but the akutubarun (creatures of mysterious origin) will try to stop them at all costs. The player will need to use the powers granted to them by various gems to achieve their objective.
project.superpanas.title: Super Panas
project.superpanas.about.one:
Super Panas is a video game developed during an intense 72-hour Game Jam, where I took on the role of Gameplay Programmer. The project was born with the dual objective of competing in the Game Jam and serving as the final project for our Unity subject at the university.

Our team achieved the highest grade among all participants, being rewarded by the judge (our professor) with a special mention (and a Pokémon figure as a trophy!).
project.superpanas.about.two:
Because of the nature of a Game Jam, I collaborated across multiple areas of development, focusing my main contributions on:

  • Character control and Game Feel: Implementation of the player movement system, polishing it with design techniques like Coyote Time.
  • Procedural generation: Design and implementation of a modular platform generation system for the fast-paced flow of the game.
  • Animated UI and Game flow: Creation of the main menu and development of the Game Over screen, which features a procedural score-counting animation.

Our biggest challenge was optimizing the modular platform system to ensure it was fun, beatable, and efficient, because we implemented an object pooling system with the level segments.
project.superpanas.video.description: Local co-op platformer game with BREAD!! Will you discover the secret ending?...
